versions.label: BSB
paragraphs: 1 Now there was another famine in the land, subsequent to the one that had occurred in Abraham’s time. And Isaac went to Abimelech king of the Philistines at Gerar.

2 The LORD appeared to Isaac and said, “Do not go down to Egypt. Settle in the land where I tell you. 3 Stay in this land as a foreigner, and I will be with you and bless you. For I will give all these lands to you and your offspring, and I will confirm the oath that I swore to your father Abraham. 4 I will make your descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky, and I will give them all these lands, and through your offspring all nations of the earth will be blessed, 5 because Abraham listened to My voice and kept My charge, My commandments, My statutes, and My laws.”

context_lines: In **Genesis 26**, Isaac faces famine under the shadow of the promises first given to Abraham. The chapter shows the Lord directing, preserving, and blessing Isaac, highlighting the contrast between divine guidance and fearful self-management.
common_interpretation_lines: A common reading of this section is that Isaac stands as a deliberate contrast to Abram's earlier Egypt episode. The external pressure is similar, but here divine instruction is given directly and becomes the governing factor.
Interpreters often connect this passage with the larger theme of trust: hardship does not disappear, but the faithful response is shaped by what God says rather than by self-protective improvisation.
current_position_lines: This site's current position is that **Genesis 26:1-5** is meant to be read against **Genesis 12:10-20**. The repeated famine is not incidental. It creates a deliberate contrast between self-directed fear and obedience under divine instruction.
The point is not that faithful people avoid hardship. The point is that the governing question becomes what God has said, not what fear suggests will work.
